explanation:
Scalability is the ability of a system to handle increased load by adding resources. Vertical scaling (scaling up) means upgrading a single machine — more CPU, RAM, or storage. Horizontal scaling (scaling out) means adding more machines to distribute the work. Think of a restaurant: vertical scaling is hiring a faster chef, horizontal scaling is opening more kitchen stations.

Availability measures the percentage of time a system is operational. A 99.99% availability ("four nines") allows only ~52 minutes of downtime per year. High availability is achieved through redundancy — multiple servers, multiple data centers, and automatic failover. If one server crashes, another takes over seamlessly.
example:
```python
import time
import random
from typing import List

class LoadBalancer:
    """Simple load balancer demonstrating horizontal scaling concepts."""
    def __init__(self, servers: List[str]):
        self.servers = servers
        self.healthy = {s: True for s in servers}
        self.index = 0

    def get_server(self) -> str:
        """Round-robin with health checking."""
        attempts = 0
        while attempts < len(self.servers):
            server = self.servers[self.index % len(self.servers)]
            self.index += 1
            if self.healthy[server]:
                return server
            attempts += 1
        raise Exception("No healthy servers available")

    def check_health(self):
        for server in self.servers:
            # Simulate health check (ping, HTTP GET, etc.)
            self.healthy[server] = random.random() > 0.1  # 90% healthy

# Simulate horizontal scaling
servers = ["server-1", "server-2", "server-3", "server-4"]
lb = LoadBalancer(servers)

for request in range(10):
    lb.check_health()
    target = lb.get_server()
    print(f"Request {request + 1} -> {target}")
```
key_takeaway: Vertical scaling has a hardware ceiling; horizontal scaling adds machines linearly. Always design for horizontal scaling from day one.
try_it: Add a weighted round-robin strategy where servers with higher capacity receive more requests.

explanation:
A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple servers to prevent any single server from becoming a bottleneck. It's like a traffic officer at a busy intersection directing cars to different lanes. Common strategies include Round Robin (equal distribution), Least Connections (send to the least busy server), and IP Hash (same client always goes to the same server).

Load balancers operate at different OSI layers. Layer 4 (transport) load balancers make routing decisions based on IP and port — fast but unaware of content. Layer 7 (application) load balancers can inspect HTTP headers, URLs, and cookies for smarter routing — directing /api calls to backend servers and /static to CDN origins.
key_takeaway: Choose your load balancing strategy based on your use case: Round Robin for stateless services, Least Connections for long-lived requests, IP Hash for session affinity.
try_it: Implement a health-check mechanism that removes unhealthy servers from the pool and periodically re-checks them.

explanation:
Caching stores frequently accessed data in fast storage (memory) closer to the consumer, avoiding repeated expensive computations or database queries. It's like keeping milk in the fridge instead of driving to the farm every time you want cereal. Common caching patterns include Cache-Aside (application manages cache), Write-Through (write to cache and DB simultaneously), and Write-Behind (write to cache first, async to DB).

Cache invalidation — deciding when cached data is stale — is famously one of the two hard problems in computer science. Strategies include TTL (time-to-live), event-driven invalidation, and versioned keys. Multi-layer caching (browser -> CDN -> application cache -> database) dramatically reduces latency and database load.
example:
```python
import time
from typing import Any, Optional, Callable
from functools import wraps

class TTLCache:
    """Simple in-memory cache with TTL expiration."""
    def __init__(self, default_ttl: int = 60):
        self._store: dict[str, tuple[Any, float]] = {}
        self.default_ttl = default_ttl
        self.hits = 0
        self.misses = 0

    def get(self, key: str) -> Optional[Any]:
        if key in self._store:
            value, expiry = self._store[key]
            if time.time() < expiry:
                self.hits += 1
                return value
            del self._store[key]  # Expired
        self.misses += 1
        return None

    def set(self, key: str, value: Any, ttl: int = None):
        expiry = time.time() + (ttl or self.default_ttl)
        self._store[key] = (value, expiry)

    def invalidate(self, key: str):
        self._store.pop(key, None)

def cached(ttl: int = 60):
    """Decorator for caching function results."""
    cache = TTLCache(default_ttl=ttl)
    def decorator(func: Callable):
        @wraps(func)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            key = f"{func.__name__}:{args}:{kwargs}"
            result = cache.get(key)
            if result is None:
                result = func(*args, **kwargs)
                cache.set(key, result)
            return result
        wrapper.cache = cache
        return wrapper
    return decorator

@cached(ttl=30)
def expensive_query(user_id: int) -> dict:
    """Simulate slow database query."""
    time.sleep(0.1)  # Simulate 100ms DB call
    return {"id": user_id, "name": f"User {user_id}", "score": user_id * 10}

# First call: cache miss, slow
start = time.time()
result = expensive_query(42)
print(f"First call: {result} in {(time.time()-start)*1000:.0f}ms")

# Second call: cache hit, fast
start = time.time()
result = expensive_query(42)
print(f"Second call: {result} in {(time.time()-start)*1000:.0f}ms")
print(f"Cache stats: {expensive_query.cache.hits} hits, {expensive_query.cache.misses} misses")
```
key_takeaway: Caching trades consistency for speed. Use TTL for simple expiration, event-driven invalidation for consistency, and multi-layer caching for maximum performance.
try_it: Add a `max_size` parameter that evicts the oldest entry when the cache is full (LRU-style).

explanation:
When a single database can't handle the load, you have two options: replication (copies of the same data on multiple servers for read scaling) and sharding (splitting data across multiple servers for write scaling). Replication is like having multiple copies of a textbook — many people can read simultaneously. Sharding is like splitting an encyclopedia into volumes — different people read different volumes.

Sharding splits data by a shard key (e.g., user_id % 4 puts users into 4 databases). Challenges include cross-shard queries, rebalancing when adding shards, and maintaining referential integrity. Replication uses primary-replica patterns: writes go to the primary, reads can go to any replica. Replication lag means replicas may serve slightly stale data.
example:
```python
import hashlib
from typing import Dict, List, Any

class ShardRouter:
    """Routes queries to the correct shard based on a shard key."""
    def __init__(self, shard_count: int):
        self.shard_count = shard_count
        self.shards: Dict[int, Dict[str, Any]] = {i: {} for i in range(shard_count)}

    def _get_shard(self, key: str) -> int:
        hash_val = int(hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
        return hash_val % self.shard_count

    def write(self, shard_key: str, data: dict):
        shard_id = self._get_shard(shard_key)
        self.shards[shard_id][shard_key] = data
        print(f"Write '{shard_key}' -> Shard {shard_id}")

    def read(self, shard_key: str) -> dict:
        shard_id = self._get_shard(shard_key)
        result = self.shards[shard_id].get(shard_key)
        print(f"Read '{shard_key}' <- Shard {shard_id}")
        return result

    def get_distribution(self) -> Dict[int, int]:
        return {sid: len(data) for sid, data in self.shards.items()}

# Shard users across 4 databases
router = ShardRouter(shard_count=4)
users = ["alice", "bob", "charlie", "diana", "eve", "frank", "grace", "heidi"]

for user in users:
    router.write(user, {"name": user, "email": f"{user}@example.com"})

print(f"\nShard distribution: {router.get_distribution()}")

# Cross-shard query requires hitting all shards
def find_all_users(r: ShardRouter) -> list:
    results = []
    for shard_id, data in r.shards.items():
        results.extend(data.values())
    print(f"Cross-shard query: fetched {len(results)} users from {r.shard_count} shards")
    return results

find_all_users(router)
```
key_takeaway: Replication scales reads (copies of data), sharding scales writes (splits of data). Choose based on your bottleneck — and avoid cross-shard queries.
try_it: Add consistent hashing instead of modulo-based sharding to minimize data movement when adding a new shard.

explanation:
Synchronous processing forces the caller to wait — like standing in line at a bank. Message queues decouple producers and consumers: the producer drops a message and continues, and a consumer picks it up later. This enables asynchronous processing, load leveling during traffic spikes, and reliable delivery with retry mechanisms.

Common patterns include Task Queues (background job processing), Pub/Sub (broadcast events to multiple subscribers), and Event Sourcing (append-only log of all state changes). Popular systems include RabbitMQ (traditional message broker), Kafka (distributed event streaming), and Redis (lightweight queues). Dead letter queues capture messages that fail processing for investigation.
example:
```python
import json
import time
import uuid
from collections import deque
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, Any

@dataclass
class Message:
    id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4())[:8])
    queue: str = ""
    payload: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    attempts: int = 0
    max_retries: int = 3

class MessageQueue:
    def __init__(self):
        self.queues: dict[str, deque[Message]] = {}
        self.dead_letter: list[Message] = []
        self.handlers: dict[str, Callable] = {}

    def declare_queue(self, name: str):
        self.queues.setdefault(name, deque())

    def publish(self, queue: str, payload: dict):
        msg = Message(queue=queue, payload=payload)
        self.queues[queue].append(msg)
        print(f"  Published to '{queue}': {payload}")

    def consume(self, queue: str):
        if not self.queues.get(queue):
            return
        msg = self.queues[queue].popleft()
        handler = self.handlers.get(queue)
        if not handler:
            print(f"  No handler for '{queue}'")
            return
        try:
            msg.attempts += 1
            handler(msg.payload)
            print(f"  Processed message {msg.id} (attempt {msg.attempts})")
        except Exception as e:
            if msg.attempts < msg.max_retries:
                self.queues[queue].append(msg)  # Re-queue
                print(f"  Retrying message {msg.id} (attempt {msg.attempts})")
            else:
                self.dead_letter.append(msg)
                print(f"  Message {msg.id} moved to dead letter queue")

    def register(self, queue: str, handler: Callable):
        self.handlers[queue] = handler

# Usage
mq = MessageQueue()
mq.declare_queue("order.created")
mq.declare_queue("email.send")

# Register handlers
mq.register("order.created", lambda p: print(f"    Order processed: {p['order_id']}"))
mq.register("email.send", lambda p: print(f"    Email sent to: {p['email']}"))

# Publish messages
mq.publish("order.created", {"order_id": "ORD-001", "amount": 99.99})
mq.publish("email.send", {"email": "user@example.com", "subject": "Order confirmed"})
mq.publish("order.created", {"order_id": "ORD-002", "amount": 149.50})

# Process messages
print("\nProcessing orders:")
mq.consume("order.created")
mq.consume("order.created")
print("\nSending emails:")
mq.consume("email.send")
```
key_takeaway: Message queues decouple producers from consumers, enabling async processing, load leveling, and reliable delivery with automatic retries.
try_it: Add a priority queue where high-priority messages are processed before low-priority ones.

explanation:
The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee only two of three properties: Consistency (every read returns the latest write), Availability (every request gets a response), and Partition Tolerance (system works despite network failures). Since network partitions are inevitable, you're really choosing between CP (consistent but may reject requests) and AP (always responds but may serve stale data).

In practice, most systems offer eventual consistency — given enough time without new writes, all replicas converge to the same state. Strong consistency guarantees reads always see the latest write but at the cost of higher latency. Choose based on your domain: banking needs strong consistency, social media likes can tolerate eventual consistency.
example:
```python
import time
import threading
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class EventualStore:
    """Simulates an eventually consistent distributed store."""
    name: str
    data: dict = None
    version: int = 0
    def __post_init__(self):
        self.data = {}

    def write(self, key: str, value: str):
        self.data[key] = value
        self.version += 1
        print(f"  [{self.name}] WRITE {key}={value} (v{self.version})")

    def read(self, key: str) -> Optional[str]:
        val = self.data.get(key)
        print(f"  [{self.name}] READ {key}={val} (v{self.version})")
        return val

class ReplicatedCluster:
    """Simulates replication with configurable lag."""
    def __init__(self):
        self.primary = EventualStore("PRIMARY")
        self.replicas = [EventualStore(f"REPLICA-{i}") for i in range(3)]
        self.pending_writes = []

    def write(self, key: str, value: str):
        self.primary.write(key, value)
        self.pending_writes.append((key, value, self.primary.version))

    def sync_replicas(self):
        """Simulate async replication with lag."""
        for key, value, version in self.pending_writes:
            for replica in self.replicas:
                replica.write(key, value)
        self.pending_writes.clear()

    def read_from_replica(self, replica_idx: int, key: str):
        return self.replicas[replica_idx].read(key)

# Demonstrate eventual consistency
cluster = ReplicatedCluster()
cluster.write("balance", "1000")
print("\nBefore sync — replicas are stale:")
cluster.read_from_replica(0, "balance")

print("\nSyncing replicas...")
cluster.sync_replicas()
print("\nAfter sync — replicas are consistent:")
cluster.read_from_replica(0, "balance")
cluster.read_from_replica(1, "balance")
cluster.read_from_replica(2, "balance")
```
key_takeaway: CAP forces a tradeoff between consistency and availability during network partitions. Choose strong consistency for financial data, eventual consistency for social features.
try_it: Implement a quorum-based read that requires responses from a majority of replicas before returning.

explanation:
A monolith is a single deployable unit containing all features — simple to develop and deploy initially, but becomes unwieldy as the team and codebase grow. Microservices decompose the system into small, independently deployable services, each owning its data and business logic. Think of a monolith as a department store, microservices as a shopping mall with independent stores.

Microservices introduce complexity: network calls replace function calls, distributed transactions replace ACID, and debugging spans multiple services. The key is to start with a monolith and extract services when team boundaries or scaling needs justify the overhead. Domain-Driven Design helps identify service boundaries around bounded contexts.
key_takeaway: Start with a well-structured monolith. Extract microservices when team autonomy, independent scaling, or technology diversity justifies the distributed systems complexity.
try_it: Add compensating transactions (rollback) to the orchestrator if the payment step fails after inventory is reserved.

explanation:
A Content Delivery Network (CDN) caches static assets (images, CSS, JS) at edge locations worldwide, serving content from the nearest node to the user. If your server is in New York and a user is in Tokyo, the CDN serves cached content from a Tokyo edge server instead of making a 14,000-mile round trip. This reduces latency from hundreds of milliseconds to single digits.

Edge computing goes further — running application logic at the edge. Instead of just caching static files, edge functions execute code (authentication, personalization, A/B testing) at CDN nodes. Platforms like Cloudflare Workers and AWS Lambda@Edge bring computation to the user, reducing the need for round trips to origin servers.
example:
```python
import hashlib
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class CacheEntry:
    content: str
    created_at: float
    ttl: int
    headers: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

    @property
    def is_expired(self) -> bool:
        return time.time() > self.created_at + self.ttl

class EdgeNode:
    """Simulates a CDN edge node with caching and edge logic."""
    def __init__(self, location: str):
        self.location = location
        self.cache: dict[str, CacheEntry] = {}
        self.hit_count = 0
        self.miss_count = 0

    def serve(self, path: str, origin_fetcher) -> str:
        # Check cache
        if path in self.cache and not self.cache[path].is_expired:
            self.hit_count += 1
            return f"[{self.location}] CACHE HIT: {self.cache[path].content}"

        # Cache miss — fetch from origin
        self.miss_count += 1
        content = origin_fetcher(path)
        self.cache[path] = CacheEntry(
            content=content,
            created_at=time.time(),
            ttl=300,  # 5 min TTL
            headers={"cache-control": "max-age=300"}
        )
        return f"[{self.location}] CACHE MISS -> origin: {content}"

    def run_edge_function(self, request: dict) -> dict:
        """Execute logic at the edge (before hitting origin)."""
        # Geo-based routing
        country = request.get("country", "US")
        if country == "JP":
            return {"redirect": "https://jp.example.com", "reason": "geo-redirect"}
        # Auth check at edge
        if not request.get("auth_token"):
            return {"status": 401, "reason": "missing auth"}
        return {"status": 200, "reason": "passed edge checks"}

# Simulate global CDN
def origin_fetcher(path: str) -> str:
    time.sleep(0.05)  # Simulate 50ms origin latency
    return f"Content for {path}"

tokyo_edge = EdgeNode("TOKYO")
nyc_edge = EdgeNode("NYC")

# First request: cache miss
print(tokyo_edge.serve("/images/logo.png", origin_fetcher))
# Second request: cache hit (instant)
print(tokyo_edge.serve("/images/logo.png", origin_fetcher))
print(f"\nTokyo stats: {tokyo_edge.hit_count} hits, {tokyo_edge.miss_count} misses")

# Edge function: auth check
result = nyc_edge.run_edge_function({"path": "/api/data", "country": "US"})
print(f"\nEdge auth (no token): {result}")
result = nyc_edge.run_edge_function({"path": "/api/data", "country": "US", "auth_token": "abc"})
print(f"Edge auth (with token): {result}")
```
key_takeaway: CDNs cache static content at edge locations worldwide. Edge computing executes logic at the edge, eliminating round trips to origin for auth, routing, and personalization.
try_it: Add cache invalidation to the edge node that removes cached entries when the origin content changes.

explanation:
Let's design a URL shortener like bit.ly. Core requirements: given a long URL, generate a short unique code; when someone visits the short URL, redirect to the original. Non-functional requirements: handle 100M URLs, 1000 redirects/second, 99.99% availability. This is a classic system design interview question that tests your understanding of hashing, databases, caching, and scaling.

Key design decisions: (1) Use base62 encoding of an auto-incrementing ID or hash to generate short codes. (2) Store URL mappings in a database with the short code as the primary key. (3) Cache popular URLs in Redis. (4) Use a distributed ID generator (Snowflake) to avoid database bottleneck. (5) Add rate limiting to prevent abuse.
example:
```python
import hashlib
import string
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

BASE62 = string.ascii_letters + string.digits

def encode_base62(num: int, length: int = 7) -> str:
    """Convert integer to base62 string."""
    result = []
    while num > 0:
        result.append(BASE62[num % 62])
        num //= 62
    return ''.join(reversed(result)).rjust(length, 'a')[:length]

class IDGenerator:
    """Snowflake-inspired distributed ID generator."""
    def __init__(self, machine_id: int = 1):
        self.machine_id = machine_id
        self.sequence = 0
        self.last_time = 0

    def next_id(self) -> int:
        current = int(time.time() * 1000)
        if current == self.last_time:
            self.sequence += 1
        else:
            self.sequence = 0
        self.last_time = current
        return (current << 10) | (self.machine_id << 4) | self.sequence

@dataclass
class URLMapping:
    short_code: str
    long_url: str
    created_at: float
    clicks: int = 0

class URLShortener:
    def __init__(self):
        self.id_gen = IDGenerator()
        self.database: dict[str, URLMapping] = {}  # short_code -> mapping
        self.cache: dict[str, str] = {}  # short_code -> long_url
        self.reverse_index: dict[str, str] = {}  # long_url -> short_code

    def shorten(self, long_url: str) -> str:
        # Check if already shortened
        if long_url in self.reverse_index:
            return self.reverse_index[long_url]
        # Generate unique short code
        url_id = self.id_gen.next_id()
        short_code = encode_base62(url_id)
        # Store mapping
        mapping = URLMapping(
            short_code=short_code,
            long_url=long_url,
            created_at=time.time()
        )
        self.database[short_code] = mapping
        self.cache[short_code] = long_url
        self.reverse_index[long_url] = short_code
        return short_code

    def resolve(self, short_code: str) -> Optional[str]:
        # L1: Check cache
        if short_code in self.cache:
            self.database[short_code].clicks += 1
            return self.cache[short_code]
        # L2: Check database
        if short_code in self.database:
            mapping = self.database[short_code]
            mapping.clicks += 1
            self.cache[short_code] = mapping.long_url  # Populate cache
            return mapping.long_url
        return None

    def stats(self, short_code: str) -> Optional[dict]:
        mapping = self.database.get(short_code)
        if mapping:
            return {"code": mapping.short_code, "url": mapping.long_url, "clicks": mapping.clicks}
        return None

# Demo
shortener = URLShortener()

urls = [
    "https://www.example.com/very/long/path/to/article",
    "https://docs.python.org/3/library/hashlib.html",
    "https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/README.md",
]

for url in urls:
    code = shortener.shorten(url)
    print(f"Shortened: {url[:50]}... -> https://short.ly/{code}")

# Resolve
code = shortener.reverse_index[urls[0]]
original = shortener.resolve(code)
print(f"\nResolved https://short.ly/{code} -> {original}")
print(f"Stats: {shortener.stats(code)}")
```
key_takeaway: A URL shortener needs: unique ID generation (base62 encoding), a database for persistence, a cache for hot URLs, and horizontal scaling with distributed ID generation.
try_it: Add a custom alias feature that lets users choose their own short code, with validation for length and reserved words.

explanation:
System design interviews test your ability to architect large-scale systems under constraints. The framework is always the same: (1) Clarify requirements and constraints, (2) Estimate scale (users, data, QPS), (3) Design high-level architecture, (4) Deep dive into critical components, (5) Address bottlenecks and tradeoffs. The key is not the perfect answer — it's demonstrating structured thinking, awareness of tradeoffs, and ability to scale.

Common patterns appear across problems: read-heavy systems need caching and CDNs, write-heavy systems need sharding and queues, real-time systems need WebSockets or SSE, and globally distributed systems need multi-region deployment. Practice by designing systems end-to-end, estimating capacity, and identifying the 2-3 components that need the most attention. Always discuss tradeoffs explicitly — there's no perfect architecture.
key_takeaway: System design follows a framework: clarify requirements, estimate scale, design high-level, deep dive critical components, address tradeoffs. Always discuss what breaks first and how to fix it.
try_it: Try the first problem — design a URL shortener — before looking at any solutions. Time yourself: 45 minutes is the standard interview window.

practice_challenges.title: Design a Rate Limiter
practice_challenges.pattern: Token bucket, sliding window, distributed counters
practice_challenges.hint: Consider: per-user vs per-IP limits, distributed rate limiting with Redis, graceful degradation when rate limiter fails
practice_challenges.description: Design a distributed rate limiter that supports multiple strategies (token bucket, sliding window), works across multiple server instances using Redis, and handles edge cases like clock skew and Redis failures
practice_challenges.expected_output: Rate limiter class with multiple strategies, Redis integration, and fallback behavior

practice_challenges.title: Design a Notification System
practice_challenges.pattern: Message queues, pub/sub, delivery guarantees, retry logic
practice_challenges.hint: Use a message queue for decoupling. Support multiple channels (email, SMS, push). Implement at-least-once delivery with idempotency
practice_challenges.description: Design a notification system that supports email, SMS, and push notifications. Handle 1M notifications/day with delivery guarantees, rate limiting per user, template management, and retry with exponential backoff for failed deliveries
practice_challenges.expected_output: Architecture diagram, API design, queue topology, and delivery guarantee mechanism

practice_challenges.title: Design a Chat System
practice_challenges.pattern: WebSockets, message storage, presence, read receipts
practice_challenges.hint: Use WebSockets for real-time messaging. Store messages in a time-partitioned database. Use a presence service with heartbeat for online status
practice_challenges.description: Design a real-time chat system supporting 1:1 and group chats (up to 500 members). Include: message delivery (offline storage), read receipts, typing indicators, presence detection, and message search. Handle 10M daily active users
practice_challenges.expected_output: WebSocket architecture, message storage schema, presence protocol, and scaling strategy

practice_challenges.title: Design a News Feed System
practice_challenges.pattern: Fan-out on write vs read, ranking algorithms, caching
practice_challenges.hint: Fan-out on write for users with few followers (pre-compute feed). Fan-out on read for celebrities (compute on demand). Cache the top N items per user
practice_challenges.description: Design a social media news feed like Twitter. Users follow others and see a ranked timeline. Handle 500M users with celebrity accounts (10M+ followers). Include: feed generation strategy, ranking algorithm, caching, and real-time updates
practice_challenges.expected_output: Feed generation strategy (push/pull hybrid), ranking pipeline, cache architecture, and capacity estimates

practice_challenges.title: Design a Distributed Task Scheduler
practice_challenges.pattern: Priority queues, distributed locking, exactly-once execution, partitioning
practice_challenges.hint: Use a priority queue with distributed locking (Redis/ZooKeeper). Partition tasks across workers. Implement exactly-once semantics with idempotency keys
practice_challenges.description: Design a distributed task scheduler like Airflow that supports: cron-like scheduling, one-time tasks, task dependencies (DAGs), priority queues, exactly-once execution guarantees, retry with backoff, and dead letter queues. Handle 100K tasks/day across 50 workers
practice_challenges.expected_output: Scheduler architecture, task queue design, worker coordination protocol, and exactly-once execution mechanism
